planting.note: Plant small bulbs 2–3 inches deep in very well-drained, gritty to sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Chilean oxalis is native to the rocky, high-altitude Andes of Chile and Argentina — a rock garden plant requiring excellent drainage, poor-to-lean soil, and a dry, cool winter dormancy. It tolerates significant frost when well-drained (to zone 5). The deeply folded, silver-gray, many-leafleted foliage is as ornamental as the flowers.

directions: Plant small bulbs in very well-drained, gritty to sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Chilean oxalis is the most ornamental and most widely grown of the Andean oxalis species in cultivation, prized for its extraordinary foliage as much as its flowers. The leaves are composed of numerous (up to 22) small, deeply folded, pleated, gray-green to silver-blue-gray leaflets that fold downward in the classic oxalis sleep-movement at night and in cold, opening in sun to reveal the pleated, accordion-fold texture of each individual leaflet — the whole leaf a marvel of geometrically precise botanical engineering in soft silver-gray. In late spring the compact mounds of silver foliage are covered with large (relative to plant size), satiny, clear pink to deep pink or lilac-pink, five-petaled flowers with slightly darker veining. Native to the high-altitude, gravelly Andes slopes where drainage is excellent and winters dry and cold, it thrives in well-drained rock garden conditions in zones 5–9, where the combination of silver foliage and clear pink flowers in a compact, tidy mound makes it one of the finest rock garden plants available from South America.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 2 inches deep in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun; candy cane sorrel follows the Cape winter-rainfall cycle — growing and blooming in winter and spring, going completely summer dormant. Keep bone dry in summer. The striped appearance of the closed flower buds — crimson on the reverse of petals that are white inside — creates the candy cane effect visible in cold or cloudy weather when the flowers close. One of the most visually distinctive of all Cape oxalis species.

directions: Plant small bulbs in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun; candy cane sorrel is one of the most visually striking and immediately recognizable of the Cape oxalis species — and the most aptly named. The flowers, when closed (as they are in cold or cloudy weather and at night), display the vivid crimson to red-and-white spiral stripes on the outer (reverse) surface of the petals that wrap around the closed bud in a tight, precise, spiral pattern of alternating crimson and white — exactly resembling a miniature candy cane or barber pole. When the flowers open in warm sunshine, the inside surface of the petals is revealed to be pure white, creating a satiny, pure-white, five-petaled oxalis flower. The transition between the vivid-striped closed bud state and the pure-white open flower state, occurring multiple times daily as sunlight and cloud alternates, is a constant source of small surprise and delight. A short plant producing small flowers, but the visual impact — particularly of a mass planting — is outsized for the plant's dimensions. Native to the sandy coastal fynbos of the Western Cape. Outstanding in pots and rock garden settings in zone 9–11.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Bowie's sorrel is a summer-rainfall species (unlike most Cape oxalis which follow the winter-rainfall cycle) and grows in summer and flowers in late summer and autumn (August–October in the Northern Hemisphere). It has large, attractive, dark-green, three-parted leaves and produces one of the largest and most vivid pink-rose flowers of any oxalis. More vigorous and spreading than most Cape oxalis.

directions: Plant bulbs in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Bowie's sorrel is an unusual South African oxalis that blooms in late summer and autumn rather than the spring of the winter-rainfall Cape species — a summer-rainfall species from the moister, eastern parts of South Africa — and produces what are, for the genus, notably large flowers: loose clusters of 3–5 deep rose-pink to magenta-pink, five-petaled flowers of 1.5–2 inches across (large for an oxalis) above dark-green, medium-sized, three-parted, clover-like leaves. The rich, vivid rose-pink color is deep and saturated, a warm, cheerful autumn pink that provides good late-season color in zones 8–11. More vigorous and spreading than the smaller, more delicate Cape winter-rainfall oxalis species, it builds substantial clumps in well-drained, sunny to partly shaded positions and can be used as an informal groundcover in the right conditions. It was named for James Bowie, the plant collector who introduced it to cultivation in the early nineteenth century during botanical expeditions to South Africa. An excellent autumn-blooming bulb for zone 9 gardens.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 2 inches deep in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun; hairy Cape sorrel follows the Cape winter-rainfall cycle, growing and flowering in winter and spring, then going completely summer dormant. Keep bone dry in summer. The name 'hirta' means hairy — the stems and leaves are covered with soft hairs. Many color forms exist — pink, red, lilac, purple — produced from the same bulb source. Best in containers in humid-summer climates where summer dormancy is difficult to manage in the ground.

directions: Plant small bulbs in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun; hairy Cape sorrel is a Western Cape fynbos oxalis of sprawling, somewhat untidy growth habit — the stems spreading outward and upward from the central corm in a loose, branching, hairy-stemmed mound — that produces in winter and spring an extended succession of large (for the genus), funnel-shaped to wide open, five-petaled flowers in a remarkable diversity of colors across the wild population: deep pink, rose-magenta, clear lilac, purple-mauve, salmon-pink. The flowers are somewhat larger than typical small Cape oxalis species, and the combination of the hairy, somewhat glandular stems with the large, saturated-color flowers creates an impression of lush, somewhat tactile, cottage-garden charm. It is one of the variable Cape oxalis species — seed populations including multiple color forms — and selected forms with particularly vivid or unusual colors are sometimes sold in cultivation. For the zone 9 container or rock garden winter garden, it provides an extended period of cold-season color and interest during the months when the garden most needs it.

planting.note: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or gritty soil in full sun; cactus pelargonium is summer-dormant and deciduous — losing all its leaves in summer and requiring bone-dry conditions during this dormancy. It grows and flowers in winter and spring. The succulent, woody stems are covered with persistent sharp spines derived from hardened leaf bases — giving the 'cactus' appearance. Not suited to humid-summer climates without container management.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or gritty soil in full sun; cactus pelargonium is one of the most botanically unusual and structurally distinctive pelargoniums — a winter-growing, summer-dormant succulent pelargonium from the dry, rocky, winter-rainfall areas of the southern Cape and Little Karoo, producing thick, fleshy, gray-green, branching succulent stems that are covered with persistent, sharp, cactus-like thorns derived from the hardened bases of fallen leaf stalks. During winter and spring, the stem nodes produce deeply-lobed, kidney-shaped or heart-shaped, gray-green leaves, and from the leaf axils emerge umbels of small, attractive white-to-pale-pink flowers with deep maroon-purple blotches on the upper petals — a distinctive small bicolor pattern against the white base. In summer, the leaves fall and the plant enters complete dormancy as a leafless, spiny, gray-green succulent stem — almost indistinguishable from a small cactus. The combination of the cactus-like spiny dormant structure in summer and the fresh-leaved, flowering winter plant is completely distinctive and unlike any other commonly grown pelargonium. Outstanding as a curiosity and conversation piece in the succulent or Mediterranean garden.
